title: Project Orama
subtitle: Autonomous AI Drone — Mechanical Frame Design
imageAlt: SolidWorks frequency FEA simulation of Project Orama drone
summary: A collaborative interdisciplinary project to design and build an autonomous drone capable of detecting obstacles using a Raspberry Pi camera. I served on the mechanical sub-team, designing the drone frame using PETG carbon fiber filament and generative design in Fusion 360.
designGoals: Two-layer component layout: Raspberry Pi, AI camera, and UPS on the top layer; ESC, flight controller, and battery on the bottom layer
Frame must withstand a maximum motor thrust load of 20N per arm without yielding
All structural components must be 3D-printable using PETG carbon fiber filament
Design must accommodate FPV standoffs, heated inserts, and motor mounts

designProcess.text:
The mechanical sub-team began by identifying the electronic components that needed to be housed and defining a two-layer stacking arrangement. The top layer holds the Raspberry Pi, AI camera, and UPS; the bottom layer holds the ESC, flight controller board, and battery. This separation kept the wiring manageable and the center of gravity low.

Drone arms were optimized using generative design in Fusion 360, which produced a lightweight curved geometry capable of distributing motor thrust loads across the arm cross-section. The output geometry was then cleaned up for printability and exported for FDM printing in PETG carbon fiber filament.

FEA simulations were run in SolidWorks — both a statics simulation at maximum motor thrust (20N per arm) and a frequency simulation to identify resonant modes that could cause structural failure in flight. The FEA results guided final wall thickness decisions on the arm and center plate.

media.caption: PETG carbon fiber filament used for printing
Generative design of drone arm in Fusion 360
SolidWorks statics FEA setup at 20N max thrust
Statics simulation results showing stress distribution

items: Balancing weight and stiffness: generative design iterations often produced geometries that were too flexible or too heavy, requiring manual trade-off decisions
Component clearance in the two-layer design required several layout revisions to ensure all connectors and cables were accessible after assembly
The final assembled frame exceeded the 12" diagonal target due to motor mount sizing, which ultimately prevented flight within the project timeline
Translating generative design output into a clean, printable geometry took additional post-processing in Fusion 360

media.caption: Frequency FEA — identifies resonant modes and high-stress regions

learned: How to apply generative design and FEA as part of an iterative mechanical design loop, not just as final validation
The importance of setting hard dimensional budgets early — the size overrun was avoidable with tighter upfront constraints
Cross-functional collaboration: working alongside Controls and Electrical teams changed how I thought about mechanical packaging
PETG CF printing properties, heated insert installation, and FPV hardware assembly
improvements: Define motor mount geometry earlier in the design process so the diagonal constraint can be enforced from the start
Build a modular arm system so arm length can be adjusted without redesigning the full frame
Run FEA earlier in the design loop as a design tool rather than a final check
Involve the Electrical team in layout decisions from day one to catch cable routing conflicts earlier

title: Bladesmithing — Lockback Folding Knife
subtitle: Mechanics of Materials · Spring Steel · Heat Treatment
summary: A semester-long group project for ENGR 42 (Mechanics of Materials). Designed and fabricated a lockback folding knife from spring steel, applying heat treatment and mechanical testing principles learned in class.
designGoals: Design and fabricate a fully functional lockback folding knife from spring steel
Achieve a target Rockwell hardness (HRC) appropriate for a knife blade through heat treatment
Blade must pass functional tests: chopping and shearing
Apply Mechanics of Materials principles — stress analysis, material selection, and failure modes — to the design
Document a complete heat treatment schedule (annealing, quenching, tempering) based on published data for the chosen steel

designProcess.text:
The project began with material selection. Our group chose spring steel for its combination of hardness, toughness, and availability. We researched its published heat treatment parameters to define our annealing, quenching, and tempering schedule.

The blade and lock-bar geometry were designed in SolidWorks, balancing blade length and thickness against the locking mechanism geometry. After fabrication, the heat treatment sequence was carried out: the steel was annealed to relieve internal stresses, then quenched to achieve martensite hardness, and finally tempered to reduce brittleness to an acceptable level.

Final testing included Rockwell Hardness measurement (HRC), a tensile test on a separately prepared material specimen, and functional chopping and shearing evaluations.

items: Controlling the tempering temperature precisely — over-tempering reduced hardness below target while under-tempering left the blade too brittle
Achieving a consistent surface finish after heat treatment required additional hand grinding and polishing steps
Fitting the lock-bar geometry required tight dimensional tolerances that were difficult to achieve with hand fabrication methods

learned: How the annealing–quenching–tempering sequence affects the microstructure and mechanical properties of steel
The practical difference between theoretical material properties and what is achievable through manual heat treatment
How to read and interpret a stress-strain curve from a tensile test and correlate it to material behavior
improvements: Use a temperature-controlled furnace rather than open flame for quenching to improve process repeatability
Design the lock bar with slightly more tolerance to simplify hand fabrication
Test multiple tempering temperatures on scrap material first to dial in the optimal HRC before treating the final blade

title: MATLAB / Raspberry Pi LED Matrix
subtitle: Orientation-Controlled Image Display · Sensehat
summary: Programmed a MATLAB script to display a custom image on an 8×8 LED matrix on a Raspberry Pi Sensehat. The image rotates in real-time based on the board's physical orientation, with additional joystick input handling.
designGoals: Display a custom image on the 8×8 LED matrix of a Raspberry Pi Sensehat using MATLAB
Automatically rotate the displayed image based on the physical orientation of the Raspberry Pi
Respond to joystick input on the Sensehat for additional user interaction
Document the hardware setup and software design in a project report and a user guide

designProcess.text:
The program was written in MATLAB using the Raspberry Pi and Sensehat support packages. The core logic reads orientation data from the Sensehat's onboard IMU and maps pitch and roll angles to one of four 90-degree rotation states for the image matrix.

The LED matrix accepts an 8×8 pixel color array, so the custom image was pre-scaled and encoded as an RGB matrix in the script. Joystick input was read in a polling loop and used to cycle between different display modes.

A Raspberry Pi setup guide was written to document the hardware configuration, MATLAB package installation, and steps to run the program on a fresh Raspberry Pi.

items: The Sensehat orientation sensor has some drift at rest, which occasionally caused the display to flicker between two rotation states — resolved by adding a small deadband to the angle thresholds
MATLAB's hardware communication with the Raspberry Pi introduced latency in the polling loop, requiring careful loop timing to keep the display responsive
Encoding a legible custom image within an 8×8 pixel constraint required multiple iterations to find something recognizable at low resolution

learned: How to interface MATLAB with physical hardware through the Raspberry Pi support package
Reading sensor data and translating it into real-time visual output
The importance of sensor filtering — raw IMU data without deadbanding produced a noticeably worse user experience
improvements: Implement a complementary filter or low-pass filter on the orientation data to further reduce display flicker
Move the program to Python to reduce communication latency between the host and the Raspberry Pi
Add more interactive display modes using the joystick for a richer user experience

title: MATLAB Tiny House Thermal Simulation
subtitle: Insulation Comparison via MATLAB & Simulink
summary: Used MATLAB and Simulink to simulate the thermal performance of a tiny house under real climate data, comparing multiple insulation types to identify which configuration minimized interior temperature variation.
designGoals: Build a MATLAB/Simulink model that simulates the thermal behavior of a tiny house using real climate data as input
Compare the thermal performance of multiple insulation types across different tiny house configurations
Identify which insulation type and wall assembly produces the most stable interior temperature
Present findings in a formal IEEE-format technical report

designProcess.text:
The thermal model was built in Simulink using a lumped-capacitance approach: the tiny house was represented as a thermal resistance-capacitance network, with wall insulation properties determining the resistance values and the interior air mass determining the thermal capacitance.

Real climate data (outdoor temperature over time) was used as the driving input. Different insulation materials were modeled by swapping the resistance values in the network, and the resulting interior temperature profile was simulated for each configuration.

Results were compared on the basis of peak interior temperature, temperature swing, and thermal lag. The best-performing configuration was selected and documented in an IEEE-format report.

items: Sourcing accurate thermal conductivity and density values for each insulation material required cross-referencing multiple engineering datasheets
The lumped-capacitance assumption simplifies the model significantly — real walls have temperature gradients that this approach cannot capture
Formatting the final report to IEEE standards while also keeping the technical content clear was more time-consuming than expected

learned: How to model a physical system as a thermal resistance-capacitance network in Simulink
That simulation results are only as good as the input data — garbage-in, garbage-out applied directly to the insulation property values
How to structure and write a technical report to IEEE standards
improvements: Use a finite difference or finite element model to capture temperature gradients through the wall cross-section
Include solar radiation as a heat gain input for a more complete thermal model
Compare simulation results against measured data from a real tiny house to validate the model assumptions
